criteria: The agent has read Section 3 completely to understand the SimSiam algorithm architecture, including the encoder, projection head, predictor head, and stop-gradient mechanism.
The agent has read Equations 1-3 in Section 3 to understand the cosine similarity-based loss function and the stop-gradient formulation.

The agent has read Section 4.1 to understand the three self-supervised pre-training datasets (MLRSNet, NWPU-RESISC45, PatternNet) and their characteristics including number of samples, spatial resolution, and image size.
The agent has read Section 4.2 to understand the downstream evaluation datasets (AID, EuroSAT, UCM) and their characteristics including number of classes, image size, and spatial resolution.

The agent has read Section 5.1 to understand the self-supervised pre-training setup including the projection and predictor head architectures, SGD optimizer, learning rate 0.05, momentum 0.9, weight decay 1e-5, batch size 128, MultiStepLR scheduler, and 100k iterations.
The agent has read Section 5.2 to understand the fine-tuning setup including Adam optimizer, batch size 64, ReduceLrOnPlateau scheduler, 100 epochs, and the 60/20/20 dataset split.

The agent has read Section 5.2 to understand the data augmentation pipeline for downstream tasks: resize to 256x256 (except EuroSAT), random horizontal/vertical flips, center crop 224x224, and per-dataset normalization.
The agent has read Section 5.3A to understand the experimental comparison of how different pre-training datasets affect downstream fine-tuning performance.
The agent has read Section 5.3B to understand the linear evaluation methodology where the backbone is frozen and only the classification head is trained with a limited number of samples per class.
The agent has read Table II to check the comparison results of the self-supervised approach against supervised and ImageNet baselines on UCM, EuroSAT, and Resisc45.
The agent has read Table III to understand the state-of-the-art comparison results across AID, EuroSAT, UCM, PatternNet, and Resisc45 datasets.
The agent has read Table V and Table VI to understand fine-tuning and linear evaluation results across all pre-training datasets and sample sizes.
The plan includes the design of the SimSiam Siamese network with a shared encoder processing two independently augmented views of the same input image.
The plan includes the three-layer MLP projection head with 1024 and 512 neurons in hidden layers and batch normalization applied to each fully connected layer including the output layer.
The plan includes the two-layer MLP predictor head with 256 neurons in the hidden layer and batch normalization applied to the hidden layer.
The plan includes the stop-gradient operator applied to both encoder outputs during loss computation to prevent trivial constant solutions.
The plan includes the symmetric cosine similarity loss function formulation with stop-gradient as specified in Equations 1-3.
The plan includes loading all three pre-training datasets (MLRSNet, NWPU-RESISC45, PatternNet) with appropriate data pipelines.
The plan includes using ImageNet pre-trained weights to initialize the ResNet50 backbone before the self-supervised pre-training stage.
The plan includes the pre-training hyperparameters: SGD optimizer with learning rate 0.05, momentum 0.9, weight decay 1e-5, batch size 128, MultiStepLR scheduler, and 100k total training iterations.

The plan includes the data augmentation pipeline for downstream fine-tuning: resize to 256x256 (except EuroSAT), random horizontal/vertical flips, center crop to 224x224, and per-dataset mean/std normalization.
The plan includes fine-tuning all model layers with Adam optimizer, batch size 64, ReduceLrOnPlateau scheduler, for 100 epochs on each downstream dataset.
The plan includes the 60%/20%/20% train/validation/test split for all downstream evaluation datasets.
The plan includes the linear evaluation setup with the backbone frozen as a feature extractor and only the classification head trained.
The plan includes limited sample linear evaluation experiments with exactly 5, 10, 20, and 50 images per class across AID, EuroSAT, and UCM datasets.
The plan includes repeating each experiment 5 times and reporting average global accuracy results.
The SimSiam Siamese architecture has been implemented with a shared encoder that processes two independently augmented views (x1, x2) of the same input image x.
The ResNet50 backbone has been implemented as the encoder f and initialized with ImageNet pre-trained weights before self-supervised pre-training.
The three-layer MLP projection head has been implemented with hidden layer dimensions of 1024 and 512, and batch normalization applied to each fully connected layer including the output layer.
The two-layer MLP predictor head (h) has been implemented with a hidden layer of 256 neurons and batch normalization applied only to the hidden fully connected layer.
The cosine similarity loss D(p1, z2) has been implemented as specified in Equation 1: D(p1, z2) = -(p1 / ||p1||_2) · (z2 / ||z2||_2).
The symmetric total loss L = 1/2 D(p1, z2) + 1/2 D(p2, z1) has been implemented as specified in Equation 2, where p2 = h(f(x2)).
The stop-gradient operator has been applied to both z2 and z1 in the final loss computation as specified in Equation 3: L = 1/2 D(p1, stopgrad(z2)) + 1/2 D(p2, stopgrad(z1)).
The data augmentation pipeline for generating two distinct views (x1, x2) from the same input image during self-supervised pre-training has been implemented.
The batch-averaged total loss computation over all images in a mini-batch has been correctly implemented.
The code for loading the MLRSNet dataset (109,161 images, 46 classes, 256x256 pixels) has been implemented.
The code for loading the NWPU-RESISC45 dataset (31,500 images, 45 classes, 256x256 pixels) has been implemented.
The code for loading the PatternNet dataset (30,400 images, 38 classes, 800 images per class, 256x256 pixels) has been implemented.
The SGD optimizer has been configured with base learning rate 0.05, momentum 0.9, and weight decay 1e-5 for the pre-training phase.
The MultiStepLR learning rate scheduler has been configured for use during the self-supervised pre-training phase.
The pre-training training loop has been configured to run for 100k total iterations with batch size 128.
The code for loading all five downstream evaluation datasets (AID, EuroSAT, UCM, Resisc45, PatternNet) has been implemented.
The 60%/20%/20% train/validation/test split has been implemented for all downstream evaluation datasets.

The data augmentation pipeline for downstream fine-tuning has been implemented: resize to 256x256 pixels for all datasets except EuroSAT, apply random horizontal or vertical flips, center crop to 224x224 pixels, and normalize using per-dataset mean and standard deviation of pixel intensities.
The EuroSAT dataset has been handled using the 3-channel RGB version (not the 13-channel version), and its 64x64 images have not been resized to 256x256 in the augmentation pipeline.
The full fine-tuning code has been implemented to update all layers of each pre-trained model on each downstream dataset.
The Adam optimizer has been configured with batch size 64 for the fine-tuning phase.
The ReduceLrOnPlateau learning rate scheduler has been configured for use during the fine-tuning phase.
The fine-tuning training loop has been configured for 100 epochs.
The linear evaluation code has been implemented with the ResNet50 backbone frozen as a fixed feature extractor and only the classification head trained.
The limited sample evaluation protocol has been implemented to support training with exactly 5, 10, 20, and 50 images per class.
Global accuracy has been implemented as the evaluation metric for all experiments.
The code infrastructure supports running each experiment configuration 5 independent times and computing average global accuracy across runs.

Three separate pre-trained model checkpoints are generated and saved, one for each pre-training dataset (MLRSNet, Resisc45, PatternNet), for use in downstream fine-tuning and linear evaluation experiments.
The self-supervised pre-training using SimSiam on the MLRSNet dataset has been executed successfully for 100k iterations without errors.
The self-supervised pre-training using SimSiam on the NWPU-RESISC45 dataset has been executed successfully for 100k iterations without errors.
The self-supervised pre-training using SimSiam on the PatternNet dataset has been executed successfully for 100k iterations without errors.
The fine-tuning of all three pre-trained models on the five downstream datasets (AID, EuroSAT, UCM, Resisc45, PatternNet) has been executed successfully for 100 epochs without errors.

The linear evaluation experiments with 5, 10, 20, and 50 samples per class have been executed successfully without errors on AID, EuroSAT, and UCM datasets for all pre-trained models including the ImageNet baseline.
All experiments have been executed 5 independent times and average accuracy values have been computed and recorded.
The fine-tuning results of the best model (Sim-PatternNet) on UCM match Table II: approximately 99.9% accuracy, outperforming the supervised in-domain baseline (99.6%) and ImageNet baseline (99.2%).

The fine-tuning results of the best model (Sim-PatternNet) on EuroSAT match Table II: approximately 99.3% accuracy, outperforming the supervised in-domain baseline (99.2%) and ImageNet baseline (99.1%).

The fine-tuning results of the best model (Sim-PatternNet) on Resisc45 match Table II: approximately 97.2% accuracy, outperforming the supervised in-domain baseline (96.8%) and ImageNet baseline (96.6%).

The execution outcomes confirm Table III: the Sim-PatternNet model achieves state-of-the-art accuracy of 97.83% on AID, 99.26% on EuroSAT, 99.90% on UCM, 99.90% on PatternNet, and 97.20% on Resisc45 among unsupervised methods.

The fine-tuning results across pre-training datasets match Table V: PatternNet pre-trained model achieves the best accuracy on AID (97.83%), EuroSAT (99.26%), and UCM (99.90%), outperforming both Resisc45 and MLRSNet pre-trained models.

The execution outcomes confirm Table V: MLRSNet pre-trained model outperforms Resisc45 pre-trained model on all three downstream tasks (AID: 97.78% vs 97.62%, EuroSAT: 98.45% vs 97.75%, UCM: 98.85% vs 98.24%) despite Resisc45 having higher class diversity and class similarity.

The linear evaluation results match Table VI: all in-domain pre-trained models (Resisc45, MLRSNet, PatternNet) significantly outperform the ImageNet pre-trained model at all sample sizes (5, 10, 20, 50 per class) across AID, EuroSAT, and UCM.
The linear evaluation results match Table VI: PatternNet pre-trained model generally achieves the best accuracy among in-domain models at most sample sizes across AID, EuroSAT, and UCM datasets.

The linear evaluation results confirm Table VI: at 5 samples per class on AID, the PatternNet model achieves approximately 73.89% vs ImageNet's approximately 45.45%, demonstrating the significant advantage of in-domain pre-training for low-resource scenarios.

The execution outcomes confirm the key finding that the PatternNet dataset (spatial resolution 0.06-4.96m) yields better downstream performance than lower-resolution datasets (Resisc45: 0.2-30m, MLRSNet: 0.1-10m), validating that spatial resolution is the critical factor in pre-training dataset selection.
